pattern: Treat integers 1..n as a 10-ary prefix tree; compute how many integers fall under each prefix subtree to skip entire branches, reaching the kth node without enumerating every element.
hints: Think of all integers 1..n as leaves of a 10-ary trie where each prefix is a node; the kth lexicographic integer is the kth node in DFS pre-order of that trie.

For a current prefix p, count how many integers in [p, n] share that prefix by iterating levels: at each level the range is [p*10, p*10+9] capped at n+1; if this count is ≥ k move into the subtree, otherwise skip the whole subtree.

Start at prefix=1; count nodes under it; if count < k, advance to the next sibling (prefix++) and subtract the count from k; if count >= k, descend (prefix*=10) and decrement k by 1 (we visited this node).

edge_inputs.description: k equals n, last in lex order
n with no trailing zeros in lex position
large n near power of 10 boundary
k skips past several top-level prefixes

stress.description: moderate n up to 50000 so the brute-force oracle stays fast
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randrange(1, min(size, 50000) + 1)
    k = rng.randrange(1, n + 1)
    return [n, k]
